findings.explanation: Neither the cluster nor a module variable exposes preferred_maintenance_window. Without it, AWS may apply engine patches and maintenance at any time, including business hours, and maintenance on Aurora clusters can trigger a brief failover. preferred_backup_window is already handled as a variable with a sensible off-peak default (04:00-09:00) elsewhere in this module — maintenance window has no equivalent.
findings.fix:
variable "preferred_maintenance_window" {
  type        = string
  description = "Preferred maintenance window"
  default     = "sun:03:00-sun:04:00"
}

# in aws_rds_cluster.default.tf:
  preferred_maintenance_window = var.preferred_maintenance_window
findings.remediation: Add a preferred_maintenance_window variable (e.g. default "sun:03:00-sun:04:00") and set it on aws_rds_cluster.default, following the same pattern already used for preferred_backup_window.
findings.title: aws_rds_cluster.default has no preferred_maintenance_window

findings.explanation: var.engine's validation block advertises support for aurora-mysql, aurora-postgresql, mysql, and postgres (variables.tf), but this resource hardcodes family = "aurora5.7" (an Aurora MySQL family) with no variable and no relationship to var.engine at all. Any consumer who sets engine to aurora-postgresql, mysql, or postgres gets a parameter group whose family doesn't match the cluster's actual engine, which AWS will reject at apply time. The resource's Terraform-local name ("examplea") and its name = "rds-cluster-pg" are also hardcoded, so two instantiations of this module in the same account/region collide on the parameter group name.
findings.remediation: Derive family from var.engine/var.engine_version (e.g. a lookup map from engine name to family prefix), and parameterize the parameter group name (or switch to name_prefix) so multiple module instances don't collide.
findings.title: aws_rds_cluster_parameter_group hardcodes family/name with no variables — breaks for 3 of the 4 supported engines

findings.explanation: aws_rds_cluster_instance.monitoring_interval is a number in the AWS provider schema, but the module declares var.monitoring_interval as type = string with default = 1 (a bare number Terraform silently coerces). This means the module's own type system doesn't actually constrain the input to numeric values the way it looks like it should — a non-numeric string passes the declared type check and only fails later, either in the validation regex or as an opaque provider error, instead of failing cleanly at the variable boundary.
findings.title: var.monitoring_interval is declared type = string but the AWS provider attribute is a number

findings.explanation: var.instances only requires length(var.instances) > 0 (variables.tf). Aurora's HA model depends on having at least one reader instance for the writer to fail over to; a caller who supplies exactly one instance (a legitimate-looking, valid config) gets a cluster with no failover target and a single point of failure. This is a judgment call rather than a hard defect — single-instance dev/test deployments are a reasonable use of this module — so it's flagged at low confidence rather than asserted as wrong.
findings.remediation: Consider documenting the HA implication of a single-instance var.instances in the variable's description, or adding a separate opt-in variable for environments that want the module to enforce >= 2 instances.
findings.title: Module places no lower bound on instance count, so callers can deploy a single-instance Aurora cluster with no failover target

suggested_policies.rationale: aws_rds_cluster has 9 existing security policies (encryption, access, backup, IAM) plus network-security proposals from a prior holden-ai-review pass (HLD_PROPOSED_001/002), but nothing checks preferred_maintenance_window. Confirmed to fire against this repo's aws_rds_cluster.default, which has no maintenance window set.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_003
name: RDS and Aurora clusters should specify a preferred maintenance window
principle: reliability
severity: LOW
rationale: |
  Without preferred_maintenance_window, AWS may apply engine patches and
  maintenance at any time of day, including during peak business hours.
  Maintenance operations on RDS/Aurora clusters can trigger a failover or
  brief unavailability, so leaving the window unset trades a small amount
  of setup effort for unpredictable-timing outages.
remediation: |
  Set an explicit off-peak maintenance window:

    resource "aws_rds_cluster" "example" {
      preferred_maintenance_window = "sun:03:00-sun:04:00"
    }
resources:
  aws_rds_cluster:
    path: preferred_maintenance_window
    not_null: true
